Task: What does an "implementation of `FnOnce` is not general enough" error mean?

Let's say we have a trait like the following, which is a distillation of how view types work in protobuf::Message:

trait Message {
    type View<'a>;
}

We are implementing an RPC system, so we define a trait for functions that know how to deal with an incoming message. (In reality we need this to make more requirements on the result type, like that it be Future + Send).

trait HandlerFn<Request> {}

impl<Request, F, R> HandlerFn<Request> for F
where
    Request: Message,
    F: Fn(Request::View<'_>) -> R,
{
}

We try to check whether the simplest possible handler implements this trait:

fn check_handler_fn<Request>(_: impl HandlerFn<Request>) {}

fn foo<Request: Message>() {
    check_handler_fn::<Request>(|_| -> () { todo!() });
}

But this fails to build, with the following not-very-helpful error message:

error: implementation of `FnOnce` is not general enough
  --> src/lib.rs:17:5
   |
17 |     check_handler_fn::<Request>(|_| -> () { todo!() });
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ implementation of `FnOnce` is not general enough
   |
   = note: closure with signature `fn(<Request as Message>::View<'0>)` must implement `FnOnce<(<Request as Message>::View<'_>,)>`, for some specific lifetime `'0`...
   = note: ...but it actually implements `FnOnce<(<Request as Message>::View<'1>,)>`, for some specific lifetime `'1`

(Playground)

What does this error mean? How can I repair the problem, teaching the compiler that the closure doesn't care about a particular lifetime?

Task: What idioms exist to make extensible options structs in Rust?

Let's say I maintain an API with a bunch of options, and that set of options may grow over time. I'd like to give a convenient way for users to override some but not all options, but without restricting me from adding new options in the future with suitable defaults. It should be convenient to set options, but ideally not require a ton of boilerplate on my part. What idioms exist for doing this in Rust?

As someone coming from C++ where I would just make this a normal struct with suitable defaults, I'm tempted to reach for struct update syntax for the user-facing aspect of conveniently overriding only some default options:

MyType::new(MyTypeOptions{
    a: 1,
    c: 2,
    ..Default::default()
})

If all users wrote it this way I'd be fine, and could add new fields. But I can't require them to do this, as far as I can see. So then I'm tempted to reach for non_exhaustive, which sounds like it solves exactly this problem. Except, annoyingly, it prevents the syntax above. This article discusses the problem, and at the bottom hints at an answer to my question, but is rather cryptic and doesn't actually spell out the idiom.

let file = OpenOptions::new()
  .append(true)
  .create(true)
  .open("logfile.log")
  .unwrap();

let subscriber = Registry::default()
  .with(
    // stdout layer
    fmt::layer()
      .compact()
      .with_ansi(true)
      .with_filter(filter::LevelFilter::from_level(Level::INFO)),
  )
  .with(
    // File layer
    fmt::layer()
      .with_ansi(false)
      .with_writer(file)
      .with_filter(filter::LevelFilter::from_level(Level::INFO)),
  );

tracing::subscriber::set_global_default(subscriber).unwrap();

I want to be able to dynamically change the log level. I have tried looking at: https://docs.rs/tracing-subscriber/latest/tracing_subscriber/reload/index.html and How can I change the log level on the fly with tracing? and also some examples in src/reload.rs in the library.

let filtered_layer = fmt::Layer::default().with_filter(filter::LevelFilter::INFO);
let (filtered_layer, reload_handle) = reload::Layer::new(filtered_layer);
tracing_subscriber::registry()
  .with(filtered_layer)
  .init(); // Panic here
reload_handle.modify(|layer| *layer.filter_mut() = filter::LevelFilter::ERROR);

let filtered_layer = fmt::Layer::default().with_filter(filter::LevelFilter::ERROR);
let (filtered_layer, reload_handle) = reload::Layer::new(filtered_layer);

If I under stand correctly this should fetch all layers which have a LevelFilter::INFO filter, then these can be modified? I get a panic on the 3rd line that the default can only be set once per program lifetime. Is it possible to change the level of only 1 of the layers, like only the console. When omitting the 3rd line the code executes but the level has not changed.

prompt:
Task: Why can Rust infer the static lifetime for a trait implementation's associated type in some syntactic situations but not others?

Here is a small example that seems to show that Rust will infer that a type associated with a trait implementation that is 'static is itself 'static:

fn require_static<T>()
where
    T: 'static,
{
}

fn foo<F>()
where
    F: Future + 'static,
{
    require_static::<F::Output>();
}

(Playground)

This compiles fine. And I guess it makes sense: if the future has no way to refer to any lifetime but the static one, how could its output know of any other lifetime itself? (Although…)

Okay, but what about this example?

fn bar<T, F>()
where
    F: Future<Output = T> + 'static,
{
    require_static::<T>();
}

This one seems like it should be morally equivalent. We have required that T and F::Output are the same type, so the fact that F::Output was 'static in foo should imply that T is here. But it doesn't work:

error[E0310]: the parameter type `T` may not live long enough
  --> src/lib.rs:24:5
   |
24 |     require_static::<T>();
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |     |
   |     the parameter type `T` must be valid for the static lifetime...
   |     ...so that the type `T` will meet its required lifetime bounds

This error persists into an even stranger case. If you modify bar minimally so that it doesn't even check T at all, only F::Output, we can see that the compiler fully well knows that T is the same as F::Output, because it complains about T even though we used F::Output in the check!

fn baz<T, F>()
where
    F: Future<Output = T> + 'static,
{
    require_static::<F::Output>();
}

error[E0310]: the parameter type `T` may not live long enough
  --> src/lib.rs:33:5
   |
33 |     require_static::<F::Output>();
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |     |
   |     the parameter type `T` must be valid for the static lifetime...
   |     ...so that the type `T` will meet its required lifetime bounds

What are the rules that govern inference of lifetimes in this situation, and how do we explain the weird behavior around foo working but not bar or baz?

I have my enum:

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
#[repr(u16)]
pub enum DataPackageSize {
    BYTES32,
    BYTES64,
    BYTES128,
    BYTES256,
}

From that, I want to be able to print a string depending on the value of the u16 variable:

// params.data_size is a u16
trace!("    Data package size: {}", DataPackageSize::from(params.data_size));

I thought that would be fairly easy - from a u16, return a str:

impl From<u16> for DataPackageSize {
    fn from(t: u16) -> &'static str {

Task: Does Rust implicitly add bounds to generic lifetime parameters?

Let's say I have this generic function that's equivalent to HashMap::get, but it accepts the key type directly instead of K: Borrow<Q> for simplicity:

fn map_get<'map, 'query, K: Hash + Eq, V>(
  map: &'map HashMap<K, V>,
  query: &'query K
) -> Option<&'map V> {
  unimplemented!()
}

Now I define a key type with a generic lifetime parameter, and a version of map_get with concrete types that uses the key type:

#[derive(PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
struct StrWrap<'a>(&'a str);

fn map_get_concrete<'map, 'query, 'a, 'b>(
  map: &'map HashMap<StrWrap<'a>, usize>,
  query: &'query StrWrap<'b>,
) -> Option<&'map usize> {
  map_get(map, query)
}

This won't compile (as of Rust v1.88), as Rust insist that 'b should outlive 'map:

error: lifetime may not live long enough
  --> src/main.rs:78:5
   |
74 | fn map_get_concrete<'map, 'query, 'a, 'b>(
   |                     ----              -- lifetime `'b` defined here
   |                     |
   |                     lifetime `'map` defined here
...
78 |     map_get(map, query)
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ function was supposed to return data with lifetime `'map` but it is returning data with lifetime `'b`
   |
   = help: consider adding the following bound: `'b: 'map`

Why is map_get(map, query) returning data with 'b here, when its definition only mentions 'map in the return type explicitly, and that K and V are separate generic types?

prompt:
Task: What's the best way to handle a callback (wiith named type) that might be unsafe if misused?

I'm designing an API that provides a type for which the user provides both a value, and a function that's supposed to run on that value – an example of the same design from the (unstable) Rust standard library is DropGuard, which takes a value T and a function F to eventually run on it.

As usual in Rust, I'm planning to make my API generic as to which function is provided (in Rust's normal monomorphic way, not erased with dyn). However, unlike DropGuard which is often only used for a short period of time, my types stick around for a while and it is likely that the user of the API might want to name them (along the lines of MyType<T, F> with concrete values substituted in for T and F). In current stable Rust, you can't name the type of a closure or function (unboxed_closures is not stable yet), and you can't implement FnOnce except with a closure or function – so instead of defining F: FnOnce(T), I am planning to bound F with a trait that provides one method, and consumes a T. This trait is effectively identical to FnOnce(T) except that it's possible to implement it on nameable types. Let's call it Consumer<T>.

The problem that has arisen is that I expect that many users will want to provide a callback that can't be run safely on every possible T value – only on the specific value that they substitute in. (An analogous situation is putting a DropGuard around a raw pointer, that dereferences the pointer when dropped: the user of the DropGuard can know that dereferencing that particular raw pointer is sound, even though dereferencing arbitrary raw pointers is unsound in general.) Although doing this sort of thing is rare in Rust generally, I expect it to be common in my particular case (it's come up already in several cases where I want to use the API). I can see three main approaches, all of which seem a little unsatisfactory:

  1. Declare Consumer as a safe trait, with a safe method. Then provide a guarantee in my API, specified as being appropriate for unsafe code to use, that the provided F will only ever be called on the provided T. This is simple to use and understand, but has the problem that it would require the user to implement a safe method that couldn't soundly be called with every possible value of the type, and that seems to violate the usual soundness rules that are used to ensure that Rust code is safe.

    In this situation, the user would be providing values of F that would in effect have a broken library/soundness invariant (the same sort of thing as a Vec whose length field is greater than the number of initialized elements, or a String that is not valid UTF-8) – although it is possible to use them soundly from code that is aware that they don't follow Rust's normal rules, they can exhibit undefined behavior when used in combination with Rust operations that are normally considered safe (in this case, calling safe methods on them). It is possible to make use of broken library invariants internally when implementing something, but encouraging the user of a public API to do that seems to me to be well out of the range of usual Rust development practices. That said, this API would produce the cleanest/simplest code and could be explained using sufficient safety comments.

  2. Declare Consumer as a safe trait with an unsafe method. Calling my API would also be unsafe, with a safety condition that "this F is sound to call on this T".

    This is easy to understand and doesn't involve any invariant violations, but has the major drawback that it requires an unsafe block even if F does in fact happen to be a safe function that can work with all values of T. It seems like a poor usability decision for an API to force people to write unsafe even in cases where there are no safety conditions that need checking (especially given that many Rust programmers avoid writing unsafe code on principle – I like to do that whenever it's reasonably possible, although it isn't always).

  3. Design the API as in 2, except provide two versions of the Consumer trait, which are identical except that one defines the method as safe and the other defines it as unsafe. Create two entry points to the API, a safe one which takes an F that implements the safe version of the trait, and an unsafe one which takes an F that implements the unsafe version of the trait.

    The problem with this approach is that – because I want my API to be able to generalise over both the safe and unsafe version of the function – I would need to provide a wrapper type that can wrap the safe value so that it implements the unsafe trait (doing this is clearly better than the other way round). So a user who provide an F implementing SafeConsumer<T> to the API would be given a MyType<T, SafeWrapper<F>> as the result from the API. This composes well and has no safety issues, but I consider needing two traits and a wrapper type to solve an apparently simple problem to be an API design flaw of its own (e.g. Java is frequently mocked due to the tendency of its programmers to do this sort of thing).

    A couple of other problems with this approach are that the type name becomes very bulky in the safe use cases (as the user will need to write SafeWrapper<F> rather than just F) and the problem of trying to find names for all the newly added types.

    I also tried the approach of blanket-implementing the unsafe version of the trait on everything that implements the safe version, in order to avoid the wrapper type, but this doesn't work due to a shortcoming of the Rust trait solver that causes it to incorrectly conclude that a downstream implementation could conflict with the blanket implementation (despite the fact that such implementations would be banned by the coherence rules).

I'm currently leaning towards option 3, but if option 1 is considered less bad than I expect it to be, I might choose that option instead. (As such, this is basically a question about "which of these practices that I consider to be bad is the least bad?", which is why I've asked it as an opinion-based question.) I'm also interested in whether there might be another option that I've missed.

prompt:
Task: How does provenance work in a kernel in Rust?

In my kernel, I receive a physical pointer to a device tree through the C ABI, should I make the type of this parameter a pointer or a usize? If I use a pointer, does Rust assume I have provenance over the memory the pointer accesses, and will it assume I still have provenance if I offset the pointer to its respective virtual memory address that I've mapped it too even if that offset is greater than isize::MAX (which it could be)? If I use a usize, and map it to virtual memory, will the pointer I create from a usize as pointer cast have the exposed provenance it needs since the device tree fits the requirements of:

memory which is outside the control of the Rust abstract machine (MMIO registers, for example) is always considered to be accessible with an exposed provenance, so long as this memory is disjoint from memory that will be used by the abstract machine such as the stack, heap, and statics.

If so, what if I later use the memory where the device tree was as part of my heap? Would that violate the condition that the memory be "disjoint"?

Say I use the device tree's information to make a memory allocator over all of physical memory, how do I provide provenance to the pointers which I return from my memory allocator? If the provenance is a subsection of the provenance of the physical memory allocator's pointer, then will the physical memory allocator be limited to only encompassing isize::MAX bytes? As the provenance of a pointer is only over an allocation, which is limited to isize::MAX.

If my kernel just must have UB, or must just use exposed provenance, should I not attempt to use any strict provenance APIs since they won't have any strict provenance anyway, or should I just use them where I can?

Task: "field must implement `Copy`" in union on field with derived Copy

Erring code in question:

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct OfflineWord {
    word: u64,
    flag: u64,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct OnlineWord<'file, F: AsRef<Path>> {
    file: &'file F,
    byte_offset: u64,
}

union FileWord<'file, F: AsRef<Path>> {
    online: OnlineWord<'file, F>,
    offline: OfflineWord,
}

Note that word in OfflineWord is in reality a Newtype but I have replaced it here since the newtype only represents processing and this way the snippet is MRE.

The file field is intended to stored a shared reference to a valid system path so it can be reused to open a file for all instances of OnlineWord. In other words, all words share a single origin file.

Whole error message:

error[E0740]: field must implement `Copy` or be wrapped in `ManuallyDrop<...>` to be used in a union
  --> src/rspeer_freq.rs:20:5
   |
20 |     online: OnlineWord<'file, F>,
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
   = note: union fields must not have drop side-effects, which is currently enforced via either `Copy` or `ManuallyDrop<...>`
help: wrap the field type in `ManuallyDrop<...>`
   |
20 |     online: std::mem::ManuallyDrop<OnlineWord<'file, F>>,
   |             +++++++++++++++++++++++                    +

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0740`.

As seen the structs implement Copy by derive and each field does so too (including &T) but compiler is still complaining about them not having Copy (or ManuallyDrop).

rustc --explain E0740 Gives a slightly different explanation, saying that no fields in a union may have destructors. Though I have no powers over that aside from not implementing them, Copy should automatically deal with that by just forgetting the memory AFAIK.

The generic type F in OnlineWord does not have a requirement for Copy but that should not be a problem since 1. derive(Copy) is not complaining 2. &T, as mentioned, has blanket Copy impl. Including Copy as a requirement removes the error but in my understanding it should not be there in the first place.

What am I doing wrong/Misunderstanding here, or is this a bug/limitation within the compiler?

A better way of doing this is encouraged but not required for answering this post.

prompt:
Task: What exactly is a context of an expression?

Up until now, I thought of a context as a place in the code that expects a certain kind of expression. The Rust Reference on Expressions writes for example that:

The following contexts are place expression contexts:

  • The left operand of a compound assignment expression. [...]

So a context is a part of an expression (e.g. the operand).

But below there is a section on mutable place expressions, and there it is said that:

The following expressions can be mutable place expression contexts:

  • Mutable variables which are not currently borrowed.
  • Mutable static items.
  • Temporary values.
  • Fields: this evaluates the subexpression in a mutable place expression context.
  • Dereferences of a *mut T pointer.
  • Dereference of a variable, or field of a variable, with type &mut T. Note: This is an exception to the requirement of the next rule.
  • Dereferences of a type that implements DerefMut: this then requires that the value being dereferenced is evaluated in a mutable place expression context.
  • Array indexing of a type that implements IndexMut: this then evaluates the value being indexed, but not the index, in mutable place expression context.

Now I'm confused: an expression (better even: an expression described as a certain type of expression) clearly is something else than an expression context. For example, a dereference of a variable *x is in itself a dereference expression. How can it be a context? To pose the question more generally, what is the way to make sense of the listed items as places in the code that can harbor place expressions?

    #[inline(never)]
    pub fn scale_backward(&mut self,
                      mf: &[u8],
                      mut hi: usize/*exclusive*/,
                      curr_stack_depth: i32,

prompt:
Task: Does a dereference operator expect a place-expression or a value-expression?

This document explains the differences between a place expression (an expression that evaluates to a "place", where something can be stored) and a value expression (an expression that evaluates to a "value", roughly something that can be put into a place).

It also talks about place expression contexts, a place where the rust compiler expects a place expression (or rather the computation thereof)?

"The operand of a unary borrow, raw borrow or dereference operator."

To me, this sounds like the operand of the dereference operator expects a place expression.

However, this blogpost explaines that the dereference operator * expects a value expression:

To consider a more complicated example, the assignment expression *ptr = *other_ptr + my_var mentioned above desugars to *(load ptr) = load *(load other_ptr) + load my_var. That’s a lot of load expressions! It is instructive to convince yourself that every one of them is necessary to make this term fit the grammar. In particular, * works on a value expression (so we need load other_ptr to obtain the value stored in this place) and produces a place expression (so we need to load again to obtain a value expression that we can use with +). However, the left-hand side of = is a place expression, so we do not load the result of the * there.

Which of those is true now? Why is there a (seemingly) contradiction in those two explanations?

Task: How to optimize enum size?

I'm building a small regex VM that matches text with bytecode-like instructions. Take the following enum:

pub type Pc = usize;

pub enum Inst {
    Char(char, Pc),
    Range(char, char, Pc),
    Any(Pc),
    Split(Pc, Pc),
    Jump(Pc),
    Match,
}

The size of this is 24 bytes (usize alignment, plus padding). But notice that the largest variants all only use 16 bytes to represent data.

As far as I understand, due to the usize alignment, the extra 1 byte that stores the discriminant has to exist in an 8 byte space. This is why the enum is so much larger than its largest variant.

But is there a way to optimize this away? It's completely unnecessary for my project, and I doubt this will affect performance in a meaningful way. I'm just curious as hell.

From references-change-permissions-on-places, places have three kinds of permission on their data:

  • Read (R): data can be copied to another location.

  • Write (W): data can be mutated.

  • Own (O): data can be moved or dropped.

In the example

fn main() {
    let mut v: Vec<i32> = vec![1, 2, 3];
    let num: &i32 = &v[2];
    println!("Third element is {}", *num);
    v.push(4);
}

From the third line, reference num gain permissions R and O but no W since it was not marked as let mut .

What can we do if a reference have permissions R and O?

I know that reference do not own its data, so how to read its data? Also, how to move a reference?

Say I have an iterator over an array of some item (i32 in this example) and I want to group them into tuples. I can do this:

fn yield_tuple<T>(it: &mut T) -> Option<(i32, i32)>
where
    T: Iterator<Item = i32>,
{
    it.next().map(|a| it.next().map(|b| (a, b)))?
}

fn main() {
    let a: Vec<i32> = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7];
    let mut it = a.into_iter();
    while let Some((a, b)) = yield_tuple(&mut it) {
        println!("({a}, {b})");
    }
}

Result:

(1, 2)
(3, 4)
(5, 6)

However, I can't put my yield_tuple function into a longer chain of iterator adapters (e.g. to collect() an array of these tuples), at least not without creating a new type and implementing Iterator or IntoIter for it, which requires a bit of boilerplate.

In other words, I'm looking for an equivalent of scan or map from std::iter that takes a closure that operates not on the items of the iterator, but on the iterator itself. That way, it could consume multiple items by calling next() before outputting something. Obviously, am happy to consider any alternative that has the same end effect.

To be clear, it's not that I always want to output tuples. It could be any object that is created from multiple (possibly even a variable number of) items from an iterator, but crucially not all items (e.g. like sum reduce and friends.)

Thanks

  • By default, binaries for both targets are fully statically-linked.
  • However, if I set -Clink-args=-fuse-ld=lld for rustc, the aarch64 binary dynamically-links interpreter /lib/ld-musl-aarch64.so.1.
  • The same setting does not affect the x86_64 binary.

Why does this difference exist? Is it possible to use lld while also getting a fully statically-linked aarch64 binary?

Steps to reproduce

# install deps
sudo pacman -Syu rustup musl musl-aarch64
rustup toolchain install stable
rustup target add {x86_64,aarch64}-unknown-linux-musl

cargo new build-test
cd build-test
mkdir .cargo
cat >.cargo/config.toml <<<'
[target.aarch64-unknown-linux-musl]
linker = "aarch64-linux-musl-gcc"'

# build without forcing lld
cargo build --target={x86_64,aarch64}-unknown-linux-musl
# observe that neither binary links interpreter
file target/*/debug/build-test

# build with forcing lld
cargo clean
RUSTFLAGS=-Clink-args=-fuse-ld=lld cargo build --target={x86_64,aarch64}-unknown-linux-musl

cargo 1.91.1 (ea2d97820 2025-10-10)

Task: Why does curl in WSL2 (Fedora and updated Arch) return "Recv failure: Connection reset by peer" on https://sh.rustup.rs?

Problem Description

I am trying to install Rust through rustup in WSL2, but in some distributions any curl request to https://sh.rustup.rs fails with a TLS error.

Command:

curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

returns:

curl: (35) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer

Environment:

  • Windows 11
  • WSL2
  • Distributions inside WSL:
    • FedoraLinux-43 (Fedora 43)
    • archlinux (Arch Linux)
  • Same router and provider, only the Linux distribution inside WSL changes.
  • On another PC, Fedora WSL2 with the same image works without any issues.

Behavior in Fedora WSL2 (problem PC)

curl -4 -v https://sh.rustup.rs output:

* Host sh.rustup.rs:443 was resolved.
* IPv6: (none)
* IPv4: 18.165.122.120, 18.165.122.110, 18.165.122.61, 18.165.122.35
*   Trying 18.165.122.120:443...
* ALPN: curl offers h2,http/1.1
* TLSv1.3 (OUT), TLS handshake, Client hello (1):
* Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
curl: (35) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer

IPv6 is not configured in WSL, so curl -6 fails with Network is unreachable (same for Fedora and Arch):

curl -6 -v https://sh.rustup.rs
...
Immediate connect fail ... Network is unreachable

Trying to restrict TLS/HTTP versions:

curl -v --http1.1 --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 https://sh.rustup.rs

still shows:

TLSv1.3 (OUT), TLS handshake
Recv failure: Connection reset by peer

Behavior in Arch WSL2 (same problem PC)

On a clean, just-installed Arch image, command:

curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

works fine --- the rustup installer script downloads normally.

However, after updating Arch:

sudo pacman -Syu

the same command returns:

curl: (35) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer

So:

  • Arch WSL2 before updates → works\
  • Arch WSL2 after updates → identical failure as Fedora\
  • IPv6 is equally unavailable on both (expected in WSL2)

What I have already tried

  • Tested various curl flags:
    • -4, -6
    • --http1.1
    • --proto '=https'
    • --tlsv1.2, --tls-max 1.2
  • Verified that Windows host browser opens https://sh.rustup.rs fine.
  • Verified clean Arch WSL2 works before updates.
  • After updates both Fedora and Arch behave the same (TLS handshake reset).
  • On another PC Fedora WSL2 works without issues.
  • The problem occurs only with:
    • https://sh.rustup.rs
    • and the Rust package registry (https://crates.io, https://index.crates.io).

Questions

  1. Why do both Fedora WSL2 and updated Arch WSL2 on this specific PC fail with Recv failure: Connection reset by peer, while clean Arch works and another PC works fine?
  2. Could this be caused by updated curl/OpenSSL and a changed TLS handshake (TLS 1.3, crypto-policies, etc.) interacting with this machine's network or security configuration?
  3. How can this issue be fixed, and what is the root cause?

Task: Cause of AlignmentMismatch error while using bytemuck?

I've also tried the slice road &[u8] -> &[T] with no avail.

My data layout

My struct, for the matter, is as follows:

#[repr(C)]
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Zeroable, Pod)]
pub struct BspLeaf {
    pub contents: i32,
    pub vis_offset: i32,
    pub mins: [i16; 3],
    pub maxs: [i16; 3],
    pub first_mark_surface: u16,
    pub mark_surfaces: u16,
    pub ambient_levels: [u8; 4],
}

Note that I'm using also others but this one is failing.

I understand that has something to do with how the struct is represented in memory, but I'm not well versed on the topic, tho. (Some terms like memory alignment and padding sound arcane to me)

Extraction method

I simply tried cast_slice, try_cast_slice, try_cast_vec with varying success rates, sometimes they work, sometimes not, which leads me to assume that I should do some data sanitization of some kind.

For example:

// Where buffer is of type Vec<u8>
let data: Vec<BspLeaf> = bytemuck::try_cast_vec(buffer)
            .map_err(|(err, _)| anyhow::anyhow!(err))?;

A little bit of background

Basically, I'm trying to parse GoldSrc BSP files for my own game, as I'm planing to use Valve Hammer or something similar for my level design.

What I've done is, following the Valve software's GoldSrc BSP data structure page, I've shaped a bunch of data structures to try to read any BSP30, in a way that the C developers would do. I'm not sure if this is the best method, but it sounded right at least.

I've tested with valid BSP files, Half-life 1 levels even, and fresh made levels (I'm trying to just open a world that is a simple box with a light and a player spawn!)

Alternatives?

If this method is too unsound, I may resort to other methods, but I'm not sure where to point to. Maybe using NOM and creating a parser somehow? Far more tedious, tho. I would like to know first why this is failing before giving up and starting all over again.

prompt:
Task: Why does std::thread::Scope::spawn need the bound `T: 'scope`?

The signature of std::thread::Scope::spawn looks like this:

pub fn spawn<F, T>(&'scope self, f: F) -> ScopedJoinHandle<'scope, T>
where
    F: FnOnce() -> T + Send + 'scope,
    T: Send + 'scope,

I understand why the requirement F: 'scope is there: the thread is only guaranteed to be joined by the time the call to scope returns, so it must not capture references with lifetimes smaller than the call to scope as it may otherwise use them from the child thread after the lifetime is exited on the parent thread.

But I'm wondering about the requirement T: 'scope. I understand this to mean that the return type of F must not include references with a lifetime smaller than the call to scope. I suppose this makes sense for a similar reason, but I don't see how this could possibly go wrong given the requirement F: 'scope. How could a function with access only to lifetimes that outlive 'scope return a reference with lifetime shorter than 'scope without the borrow checker complaining about that function in isolation, before it's even passed to spawn?

Is the T: 'scope requirement redundant?

Task: How to figure out why Rust failed to vectorize and fix it?
  • How do I know if rustc or LLVM is doing the vectorizing?
  • How can I fix this specific example?

Here's a simplified piece of extremely performance-sensitive decompression code I would like to use:

pub unsafe fn decompress_offsets(
    base_bit_idx: usize,
    src: &[u8],
    offset_bits_csum_scratch: &[u32],
    offset_bits_scratch: &[u32],
    latents: &mut [u64],
) {
    for (&offset_bits, (&offset_bits_csum, latent)) in offset_bits_scratch.iter().zip(
        offset_bits_csum_scratch
            .iter()
            .zip(latents.iter_mut()),
    ) {
      let bit_idx = base_bit_idx as u32 + offset_bits_csum;
      let byte_idx = bit_idx / 8;
      let bits_past_byte = bit_idx % 8;
      *latent = read_u64_at(
        src,
        byte_idx as usize,
        bits_past_byte,
        offset_bits,
      ).wrapping_add(*latent);
    }
}

#[inline]
unsafe fn read_u64_at(
  src: &[u8],
  byte_idx: usize,
  bits_past_byte: u32,
  n: u32,
) -> u64 {
  debug_assert!(n <= 57);
  let raw_bytes = *(src.as_ptr().add(byte_idx) as *const [u8; 8]);
  let value = u64::from_le_bytes(raw_bytes);
  (value >> bits_past_byte) & ((1 << n) - 1)
}

Godbolt with x64 and aarch64

This vectorizes on x64 but fails to do so on aarch64. I can get some very similar loops to vectorize, if I

  1. remove the final wrapping add, or
  2. write to another dst: &mut [u64] buffer instead of working in-place.

However, I would rather not do those things for performance reasons, and in reality I have several generic versions of this loop, so I can't easily write inline assembly.

Things I've tried:

  • looked at the LLVM IR. The vectorizing versions have a vector.body section, but I'm not sure if rustc produces that or LLVM does and I'm just looking at IR after all the optimization passes.
  • looked at the assembly on both platforms. It appears to me that what I want is definitely possible by tweaking the assembly from (1.) above.

So how can I get the behavior I want here?

Task: Why is a mutable reference with any lifetime not enough to make a type invariant in a lifetime parameter?

While trying to convince myself precisely why this code in std::thread::Scope works, I hit upon the following problem. Below is a struct that accepts two lifetime parameters, and a pair of functions that demonstrate that the struct is covariant over its first lifetime parameter:

struct Foo<'a, 'env>
where
    'env: 'a,
{
    marker: PhantomData<&'env mut &'a ()>,
}

// Accepts two Foo objects that must have the same lifetime.
fn accept_same<'b, 'env>(f1: Foo<'b, 'env>, f2: Foo<'b, 'env>) {}

// Accepts two Foo objects that can have different lifetimes.
fn accept_different<'a, 'b, 'env>(f1: Foo<'a, 'env>, f2: Foo<'b, 'env>)
where
    'a: 'b,
{
    // This compiles, meaning Foo must be covariant over its first lifetime
    // parameter. The lifetime of f1 is reduced to match the lifetime of f2.
    accept_same(f1, f2);
}

(Playground)

However, I'm surprised that Foo is covariant over its first lifetime parameter. Here's the proof I would have written for it being invariant over that parameter:

Consider Foo<'a, 'env> and Foo<'b, 'env>, where 'a outlives 'b. The question of whether Foo<'a> is a subtype of Foo<'b> reduces to the question of whether &'env mut &'a () is a subtype of &'env mut &'b (). We seek to show that the answer is no.

We can rewrite the types as follows:

  • &'env mut T1 (where T1 is &'a ())
  • &'env mut T2 (where T2 is &'b ())

Although it's true that T1 is a subtype of T2, this doesn't matter because those types are wrapped in a mutable reference, and mutable references are invariant in the referred-to type. Therefore there is no subtyping relation between these two mutable reference types.

But I guess the compiler disagrees with my proof, so there must be something wrong with it. Why does this subtyping work? In other words, where is the flaw in my proof that Foo is invariant over its first lifetime parameter?

prompt:
Task: How can I invoke the release-mode behavior of Rust's << operator from debug mode?

Here is a Rust program which performs the << operation on two u8 values:

#[allow(arithmetic_overflow)]

fn main() {
    println!("{}", 1_u8 << 10_u8);
}

In debug mode, this program crashes with an overflow error at runtime, because that's one of the overflow checks that is performed in debug mode. In release mode, it prints 4, because the shift amount is masked to three bits so as to not be greater than the bit length of the shifted value. (The [allow(arithmetic_overflow)] doesn't influence this behavior, it just asks Rust to go ahead and compile this code even though it knows that an error/overflow will occur at runtime; you can get the same behavior without that flag by having the values be dynamically computed.)

I would like to invoke the release-mode behavior from debug mode. That is, I'd like to write code that looks something like this, such that the masking behavior occurs no matter whether I'm compiling in release mode or debug mode:

#[allow(arithmetic_overflow)]

fn main() {
    println!("{}", 1_u8.release_mode_shl(10_u8));
}

However, I can't find such a method in u8's documentation page. The only methods I can find which refer to left-shifting are .checked_shl(), .exact_shl(), .funnel_shl(), .overflowing_shl(), .strict_shl(), .unbounded_shl(), .unchecked_exact_shl(), .unchecked_shl(), and .wrapping_shl(). All of these methods accept a u32 as their argument, not a u8, meaning they can't be the same as what happens when I use 1_u8 << 10_u8 in release mode.

So, how can I get the release-mode behavior of u8 << u8 in debug mode? Is it simply 1_u8.wrapping_shl(10_u8 as u32)? Is the answer documented anywhere?

Extra context

I need this behavior because I am writing a library which attempts to emulate the behavior of certain Rust functionality (evaluating mathematical expressions) specifically in release mode, and I would like users to be able to use this library even in debug mode. The reasons I expect it to be possible are (1) it just seems like it should be possible, and (2) the analogous thing is possible for e.g. the + operator, by using the .wrapping_add() method:

#[allow(arithmetic_overflow)]

fn main() {
    println!("{}", 200_u8.wrapping_add(200_u8));
}

This program compiles and prints 144 in both debug mode and release mode, and that's exactly what I want, but I want it for <<.

prompt:
Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

prompt:
Task: What's the idiomatic/correct way to split a string into its first word and the rest of the string?

tl;dr

Ideally, I'd want the following

  • "hello world" is split in "hello" and " world"
  • " world" is split in "" and " world"
  • "hello " is split in "hello" and " "
  • "world" is split in "world" and ""
  • "" is split in "" and ""

where the leading space(s) in the second word (which is not there anyway in case the original string contains no spaces) could as well be removed.

Research

Since Haskell has such a function, called break, I'm wondering whether Rust has one such function too. I haven't been able to dig it out from the doc, though. I've only found split_once, but it doesn't quite cut it, as you can see below. I've also come across this question where one of the answers makes me think the solution could entail .chars().collect::<Vec<char>>(), but it all starts feeling a bit of a roundabout for a simple task.

Usecase

The usecase consists in extracting the command out of a command line in a uniform way, e.g. echo from both echo and echo something, cd from both cd $HOME and cd, exit from both exit and exit 1, and so on.

My initial approach when I began to build my own shell for exercise was to use split_first, but this leads to some duplication of patterns:

match command.trim().split_once(' ') {

    // when commands are given some arguments
    Some(("exit", s)) if s.parse::<i32>().is_ok() => exit(s.parse::<i32>().unwrap()),
    Some(("echo", s)) => println!("{}", s),

    // when commands are given no arguments
    None => match command.trim() {
        "" => print!(""),
        "exit" => exit(0),
        "echo" => println!(""),
        _ => println!("{}: command not found", command.trim())
    }

    // unknown command given with some arguments
    Some((cmd, _)) => println!("{}: command not found", cmd)
}

Ideally, I'd want to deal with exit in one place only, and there branch on whether there's arguments or not (and similarly for echo and any other command).

With some AI help I came up with

fn break_on<F>(s: &str, pred: F) -> (&str, &str) where F: Fn(char) -> bool, {
    match s.find(&pred) {
        Some(idx) => s.split_at(idx),
        None => (s, ""),
    }
}

which seems to do the job, but being new to Rust, I can't really tell whether this is a good way to address the usecase.

Task: Search in BTreeMap with partial Borrow

I'm trying to store data in a BTree-like collection with a (u32, String) tuple as the key, and then be able to retrieve the data using a u32 and a &str without copying the whole string.

BTreeMap has a pretty convenient implementation of get() that lets you used borrowed data as the key even though the key has to be owned. So I can use String as a key and search with `&str', but if I try to do something similar with the tuple, I need to implement the following trait:

// Needs a local type to impl trait
struct Key(u32, String);

// Some lifetime noise to make sure the inner borrow doesn't outlive the outer one
impl<'a> Borrow<(u32, &'a str)> for Key where Key: 'a {
    fn borrow(&self) -> &(u32, &'a str) {
        &(self.0, self.1.as_str())
    }
}

But this correctly fails with:

error[E0515]: cannot return reference to temporary value
 --> src\main.rs:8:9
  |
8 |         &(self.0, self.1.as_str())
  |         ^-------------------------
  |         ||
  |         |temporary value created here
  |         returns a reference to data owned by the current function

Is there any crate that provides a functionnality similar to BTreeMap that would allow for O(log(N)) access on values without cloning the key? This could be either by letting me use the (u32, &str) to search for a value somehow, or letting me iterate without having to potentially visit all the values. For example I imagine a fn find(&self, f: F) where F: Fn(&K) -> Ordering would let me write map.find(|Key(l, r)| tuple.cmp((l, r.as_str())). Or I doubt it, but it seems a bit silly to have an allocation issue with (u32, &str) when it's a type that pretty easy to Copy around, so maybe there's still a way to implement Borrow?

Note: technically in my case the strings are pretty small, and the code is nowhere near called often enough that .clone() is a issue. Otherwise, I know that using a HashMap rather than a BTreeMap could be worth considering. The reason I started investigating with this specific set of constraints is that I started with struct Key(u32) as the key, until I realized there could some cases where the String was required to distinguish two values, and now I have to .clone() strings around just to look for an identical copy.

Task: Nested macros: using env! where LitStr is expected

The TL;DR description is that when calling outer!(inner!()) where outer expects an str litteral and inner expands to one, you still get an error because the inner macro is not a litteral before expansion.

I'm trying to use a macro that expects a string litteral (cryptify's encrypt_string!() for example) as an input, and use env!() where the litteral is expected but the latter isn't expanded soon enough and the token stream isn't parsed as a litteral. (edit: as pointed out by DanielWalker in a comment, the documentation for env! does state it generates a string, not a litteral).

fn main() {
    println!("My name is: {}", cryptify::encrypt_string!(env!("NAME_VAR")));
}

error: expected string literal
 --> src\main.rs:2:58
  |
2 |     println!("My name is: {}", cryptify::encrypt_string!(env!("NAME_VAR")));
  |

One solution is to generate the code cryptify::encrypt_string!("<actual value of the variable>") in a file in the build.rs step, then use include!() that into the code. But this means that the value that should be obfuscated ends up being stored in a file which kind of defeats the purpose.

For what it's worth, using env! works as expected as the first parameter of println!, so you can have:

// build.rs
fn main() {
    println!("cargo::rustc-env=PATTERN={}", "My pattern and values: {} {} {}")
}

// src/main.rs
fn main() {
    println!(env!("PATTERN"), 1, 2, 3);
}

and it will print My pattern and value: 1 2 3. But from what I understand I can't expect to take inspiration from that because println! is built on built-ins rather than standard macros.

Task: How do I cast a *mut T to a *mut [T]?

In Rust, both references and pointers to unsized types have metadata indicating the actual type of the reference. For example, a *mut [T] consist of a pointer to the start of a slice, plus a length that specifies how many elements are in the slice.

It's possible to convert a *mut [T] into its two components, a *mut T pointer to its first element and its length, using an as cast to get at the pointer to the first element (or by using the unstable wrapper <*mut [T}>::as_mut_ptr), and using <*mut [T]>::len() to get at the length.

But how do you go the other way? Suppose I have a pointer to a slice's first element (that has provenance covering the entire slice), and know its length, and want to produce a pointer (i.e. not a reference) to the whole slice.

I tried checking the standard API documentation for slices and pointers but didn't find anything relevant. The closest thing I found was std::slice::from_raw_parts_mut, but that returns a reference rather than a pointer.

Task: Is binding-level mutability necessary for a borrow checker, or could Rust have supported type-level const like C++?

I am new to Rust and one thing that stands out to me is that in Rust, constness/mutability is not bound to the type, but to a particular variable. So, it seems to me like it would be impossible in Rust to create a mutable container of immutable objects (e.g. std::vector<const int> in C++) without having to create a new, specialized container.

My question is, was Rust's decision to bind constness/mutability this way out of necessity to facilitate their barrowing system, or is it completely orthogonal? Basically I am asking if you could create a language with a borrow checker that still used constness as a type trait as C++ does.

Task: Drop Order Changes Randomly Between Async Boundaries

I noticed that destructors (Drop) in async functions don’t always execute in the same order when nested awaits are used.

struct Guard(&'static str);
impl Drop for Guard { fn drop(&mut self) { println!("drop {}", self.0); } }

async fn inner() { let _x = Guard("x"); }
async fn outer() { let _y = Guard("y"); inner().await; }

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() { outer().await; }

Running this several times (Rust 1.81.0-nightly, Windows 11 x64) gives:

drop x
drop y

and sometimes:

drop y
drop x

Same binary, no code changes, just inconsistent results. Is there a reason why the drop order of async stack frames is not deterministic? I can’t tell if this is expected or an optimizer artifact.

Aside from trying all sorts of different paths I have researched in many places but nowhere I could find a solution to the problem. I hope somebody here with experience in Rust GTK4 development can tell me what I am missing.

Task: Transforming a UTF-8 string in a const context

I have a function capable of translating UTF-8 characters into a custom 8-bit encoding:

const fn char_to_custom_encoding(c: char) -> u8;

I'd like to apply it at compile time to a string literal so that only the translated array is stored and can be accessed instantly at runtime, with a signature along the lines of

const fn literal_to_custom_encoding(input: &'static str) -> &'static [u8]

The source string is expected to contain UTF-8 characters, so processing it naively byte-by-byte as if it was ASCII, like done in this blog post, is not an option, and str.chars() can not be used at compile time as it is not a const method.

So far, my best bet seems to be to employ the general approach described in the aforementioned blog post and then manually detect UTF-8 characters during iteration. However, aside from having to write a manual implementation when a built-in one exists, this will also lead to the resulting array being padded with zeros as multi-byte characters get converted into single values and don't fill it completely; this is made even more complicated by the crate being no_std, making returning a Vec nonviable even at runtime.

Is there a better approach to this problem that still ensures that the transformation is done at compile time? I've considered using macros, but it seems that would leave the problem of iterating over the UTF-8 characters largely unsolved (there's the const_format crate doing something similar internally, but I'm having a hard time understanding its code) and I'd have to reimplement the conversion function as a macro as well, making it completely impossible to use at runtime even if desired.

Task: Detect leaking of private types which are only public in their module

Suppose I have a Rust crate mycrate with lib.rs:

mod internal {
    pub struct X();

    pub fn x() -> X {
        X()
    }
}

pub use internal::x;

This works fine on Rust 2024 and any consumer can use let val = mycrate::x();. However, X is not publically available making it very tricky to work with.

Question

How can I automatically detect if there are private types that my API "leaks".

Notes and clarifications

  • The best I have found to manually check this is to play around with pub(crate) rather than pub. If I use e.g. pub(crate) fn x() -> X I cannot compile due to "x is only public within the crate, and cannot be re-exported outside". Alternatively, I can use pub(crate) struct X(); which gives me the correct warning "type Xis more private than the itemx". However, this requires manual checking which is not scalable for larger projects.
  • AI tools steer me towards using cargo doc and cargo-public-api, which produce insight into the API. Here too it is not straightforward to manually check the output and see which modules are private.
  • I am looking for a tool or workflow that helps me to easily detect these cases on any source code, without requiring me to know the details of that source code.
  • While the question is generic, it primarily came up in error handling where I often encounter the case Result<PublicStruct, PrivateEnumError>.
  • I have seen the following threads, which suprisingly seem to almost express my desired solution in older versions of Rust...

    • How to reference private types from public functions in private modules?

    • Private inner module returning private item gives "private type in public interface" error

    • What's the best way to use a private type in a public function?

export def label-encode [
  column_name: string # The name of the column to encode
] {
  # Ensure the input is a table
  let input_table = $in
  let input_table = if ($input_table | describe) like "NuDataFrame" { 
      $input_table 
    } else { 
        $input_table | polars into-df
    }

  # Get unique values from the specified column using native Nushell
  let unique_values = $input_table 
    | polars get $column_name 
    | polars unique 
    | polars into-nu 
    | get $column_name 
    | sort

  # Create a mapping from unique value to an integer index
  let mapping = $unique_values | enumerate | rename $"encoded_($column_name)" $column_name | polars into-df 
  
  # Join the mapping with the original table on the specified column
  return ($input_table | polars join $mapping $column_name $column_name)
}

Task: Reusable iterator transformations

Problem:

I have a number of iterators where I perform a series of operations on them, a part of which is identical for each. What's the idiomatic way to reuse the recurrent part of the code?

I.e. I have a pattern that looks something like this:

let r1 = get_iter1()
    .flat_map(f1)
    .map(f2)
    .flatten()
    .flat_map(g1)
    .any(|x| g2(x));
let r2 = get_iter2()
    .flat_map(f1)
    .map(f2)
    .flatten()
    .flat_map(h1)
    .any(|x| h2(x));
let r3: Vec<_> = get_iter3().flat_map(f1).map(f2).flatten().collect();

How can I reuse the common part .flat_map(f1).map(f2).flatten() without duplicating the code?

Attempted solution:

Simplified example of the actual code:

let r = [(1, 2)]
    .iter()
    .flat_map(|&(i, j)| [(i, j), (j, i)])
    .any(|(i, j)| j - 1 == i);

Since the problem is local to one function, it seems obvious to try to extract the reusable bits into a lambda expression. But that won't compile:

let f = |it: std::slice::Iter<'_, (i32, i32)>| it.flat_map(|&(i, j)| [(i, j), (j, i)]);
let r = f([(1, 2)].iter()).any(|(i, j)| j - 1 == i);

The error message is

error: lifetime may not live long enough
 --> src/main.rs
  |
  |     let f = |it: std::slice::Iter<'_, (i32, i32)>| it.flat_map(|&(i, j)| [(i, j), (j, i)]);
  |              --                                  - ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ returning this value requires that `'1` must outlive `'2`
  |              |                                   |
  |              |                                   return type of closure is FlatMap<std::slice::Iter<'2, (i32, i32)>, [(i32, i32); 2], {closure@src/main.rs: 2:73}>
  |              has type `std::slice::Iter<'1, (i32, i32)>`

Which to my understanding means that the compiler cannot properly infer the appropriate lifetime for the result of f.

Is there any guarantee in Rust that lifetime parameters won't change the layout of a type? For example, is the following guaranteed to be okay as long as we ensure that the result doesn't outlive 'a?

// The caller must ensure that the result doesn't outlive 'a.
unsafe fn erase_future_lifetime<'a>(

) -> Box<dyn Future<Output = ()>> {
    let raw: *mut (dyn Future<Output = ()> + 'a) = Box::into_raw(b);

    unsafe { Box::from_raw(raw2) }
}

Router looks like this:

pub fn init_routes(app_state: AppState) -> Router {
    let shared_app_state = Arc::new(app_state);
    let (router, api) = OpenApiRouter::with_openapi(ApiDoc::openapi())
        .routes(routes!(health::health))
        .nest("/api/configitems", configitems_router())
        .nest("/api/incidents", incidents_router())

Task: How to fix lifetimes when returning and iterator over borrowed data?

I'm developing a rust crate that abstracts audio buffers. A key challenge are non continuous slices. My solution to those is the MutableView, it's supposed to remap a continuous index to a non continuous one using a mapping function.

A core part of the crate is the Buffer trait that i haven't included. It allows indexing a buffer along axis that are represented by the BufferAxis trait that i have included.

The way this works is that a frame/channel of the buffer implements BufferAxis. For non continuous frames/channels the concrete type would be View or MutableView. Therefore i need to implement BufferAxisMut for MutableView:

impl<'a, D, M, J> BufferAxisMut<D::Output> for MutableView<'a, D, M, usize, J>
where
    D: IndexMut<J>,
    M: InjectiveMapper<usize, J>,
    D::Output: 'a,
{
    fn get_sample_mut(&mut self, index: usize) -> Option<&mut D::Output> {
        self.get_mut(index)
    }

    fn iter_samples_mut<'this>(&'this mut self) -> impl Iterator<Item = &'this mut D::Output>
    where
        D::Output: 'this,
    {
        MutableViewIterMut { // lifetime may not live long enough
            view: self,      // consider adding the following bound: `'this: 'a`
            index: 0,
        }
    }
}

As I have annotated in the code block I get a compiler error, saying that:

lifetime may not live long enough
consider adding the following bound: `'this: 'a`

Firstly the compiler suggest adding a bound however that is not possible because it is not in the trait's signature and it can't be in it either because the lifetime 'a is specifcic to the MutableView struct.

Secondly the bound 'this: 'a means that 'this must outlive 'a. But isn't that obvious because 'this is the lifetime of an instance of the struct and the struct has the lifetime 'a?

Regardless of this suggestion though, I can't apply it anyways, so it seems like the pattern how I implemented this doesn't work. Is that the case?

I'd be very happy if you can solve this but I would also appreciate knowing if this is a common pattern or if there are common patterns for doing what I want. Or even a resource that explains parts of my questions.

prompt:
Task: Trait object taking ownership self

I want to build an application that interfaces with various cameras. Which specific camera is determined at runtime based on which is available. However, all cameras send frames one by one. Thus, to capture a video without blocking the caller, processing incoming frames should be done in a separate thread. Furthermore, while the camera is capturing a video, it cannot be used any other way. To encode that into types, I wanted to make a trait that that exposes a capture_video function and that gets implemented by ever concrete implementation for the various camera models and to avoid the camera being used while capturing a video, I thought it should take ownership at the start and return it when finished. So, I came up with this:

pub type Camera = Box<dyn CameraTrait + Send + Sync>;

pub trait CameraTrait {
    fn capture_video(self, video_update_callback: Box<dyn Fn(Frame) + Send>, tx: std::sync::mpsc::Sender<Camera>) -> Box::<dyn FnOnce()>;
}

When the application wants to stop capturing, it calls the function returned by capture_video which signals the camera thread to stop processing, clean up any resources needed for capturing and return the camera object via the sender provided once ready for future use. Of course, all other code should be independent of the concrete camera used, so it uses a variable of the type Camera which is just a shorthand for the trait object. However, calling camera.capture_video on this runs into the problem of not being statically sized. One could of course instead use a flag or such in the camera and return some error if the camera is busy. But encoding it into the type system feels more idiomatic rust. But I do not know how to make it work.

Does someone know a better way of encoding this?

prompt:
Task: Iterate over elements, not references on them

I am trying to implement a display driver wrapper in (no_std) Rust, that is responsible to write an sequence of pixels into a frame buffer. The pixel data is coming from the C world, and representing valid Rgb565 16bit pixels already. The pixel buffer is referred by void* pointer to the first pixel and the length is known. My intention is to create a Color::Rgb565 slice from the pixel data, and turning that into an interator and call embedded_graphics_core::draw_target::fill_contiguous() with that pixel slice.

My (unsafe) code:

if !color_p.is_null() {
    let color_p = color_p as *const Rgb565;
    let colors = core::slice::from_raw_parts(color_p, (w*h) as usize );
    let colors_it = colors.iter();
    display.fill_contiguous(&r, colors_it);
}

Unfortunately, the fill_contiguous() function has a very specific trait bound on the iterator:

    fn fill_contiguous<I>(
        &mut self,
        area: &Rectangle,
        colors: I,
    ) -> Result<(), Self::Error>
       where I: IntoIterator<Item = Self::Color> { ... }

This leads to the following compile error, due to the fact that my iterator is returning references to elements, instead of the elements themself:

40  |                     display.fill_contiguous(&r, colors_it);
    |                             ---------------     ^^^^^^^^^ expected `Rgb565`, found `&Rgb565`
    |                             |
    |                             required by a bound introduced by this call

The compilation can be fixed easily by copying the pixels by modifying the 4th line of my code snippet using copied() on the iterator and the code is compiling and working fine then:

let colors_it = colors.iter().copied();

However, this unnecessary copy is highly undesirable for performance reasons, as this code is executed for every changed pixel at the refresh rate of the display. Is there any more elegant way to deal with this issue without copying every pixel?

Task: How do I use str::split and str::split_whitespace in the arms of a match expression in Rust

I have some code that splits a string and then does some processing on each part of the string, before returning Vec of values based on each segment. By default, the code should split the string by whitespace, but the caller might want to provide an optional separator to use instead.

Here is a simplified version of the code I am trying to write:

pub fn parse_line(line: &str, separator: Option<&str>) -> Vec<String> {
    let words  = match separator {
        Some(separator) => line.split(separator),
        None => line.split_whitespace(),
    };

    words.map(|word| { String::from(word) }).collect()
}

The problem is that the split() and split_whitespace() functions return incompatible types.

 1  error[E0308]: `match` arms have incompatible types
   --> src/log_parser.rs:42:17
    |
 40 |       let words  = match separator {
    |  __________________-
 41 | |         Some(separator) => line.split(separator),
    | |                            --------------------- this is found to be of type `std::str::Split<'_, &str>`
 42 | |         None => line.split_whitespace(),
    | |                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ expected `Split<'_, &str>`, found `SplitWhitespace<'_>`
 43 | |     };
    | |_____- `match` arms have incompatible types
    |
    = note: expected struct `std::str::Split<'_, &str>`
               found struct `SplitWhitespace<'_>`

 For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0308`.

I know that I can work around this issue by collecting the results into a Vec<&str> first, but that seems wasteful. This code will be in a critical section of my program and I want it to run as fast as possible.

pub fn parse_line(line: &str, separator: Option<&str>) -> Vec<String> {
    let words: Vec<&str>  = match separator {
        Some(separator) => line.split(separator).collect(),
        None => line.split_whitespace().collect(),
    };

    words.iter().map(|word| { String::from(*word) }).collect()
}

Is there a way I can use the results of the split functions as a generic iterator over &str, so I don't have to duplicate my string processing code for each case? And is it possible to do so without creating an intermediate Vec?

Is this possible with a dynamic trait? Can I do it without allocating anything on the heap?

I've read the docs for both of these functions and their return types, but I've just started learning Rust and I don't really understand the difference between them. It seems like it might have to do with reverse iteration, but I don't need that functionality for my use case.

prompt:
Task: How to decide between async function and function that returns a future?

Let's say I want to write a function that transforms a future in some way. Maybe it causes a message to be printed with each call to poll, for example. There are two different ways to express this syntactically, and they are isomorphic in the sense that they can each be implemented in terms of the other:

async fn async_fn<F: Future>(f: F) -> F::Output {
    return_future(f).await
}

fn return_future<F: Future>(f: F) -> impl Future<Output = F::Output> {
    async_fn(f)
}

I believe async_fn can use .await expressions in its body, and return_future can't. But aside from that, are there substantive differences between these two?

  • Is there any efficiency difference between the two, in terms of allocations, inlinability, or other concerns of generate code?

  • Is there any difference in terms of flexibility for the caller in terms of how they use the result?

  • Which style is generally preferred?

Because the only difference I know between the two is "can/cannot use .await", I generally pick based on whether I need to use .await. But that is a rather unsatisfying answer, since it's all about implementation details—the function signature seems like it's an API-level concern that is a contract with the caller, and the implementation details should matter less.

IO_BUF_BLOCK_SZ = 4096
DEFAULT_IO_BUF_SZ = 256 * 1024 * 1024
MAX_PRC_VIRT_MEM = 100 * 1024 * 1024
MAX_PRC_STACK_MEM = 100 * 1024 * 1024
TIK = 0.1


def limit_virtual_memory():
    
    # The tuple below is of the form (soft limit, hard limit).
    # When the limit cannot be changed, setrlimit() raises ValueError.
    # soft, hard = resource.getrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_AS)    

    # resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_AS, (MAX_PRC_VIRT_MEM, resource.RLIM_INFINITY))
    # resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_AS, (MAX_PRC_VIRT_MEM, MAX_PRC_VIRT_MEM))
    resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_DATA, (MAX_PRC_VIRT_MEM, MAX_PRC_VIRT_MEM))
    
    if not platform.uname().system == "Darwin":
        resource.setrlimit(
            resource.RLIMIT_STACK, (MAX_PRC_STACK_MEM, MAX_PRC_STACK_MEM)

Task: How to conditionally (compile) import a group of modules based on a feature?

I always struggle a bit with the Rust/Cargo features and conditional compile.

I have a number of Functions and a number of FunctionGroups.

Let's say I have Functions

  • F_A,
  • F_B,
  • F_C

and FunctionGroups

  • FG_1,
  • FG_2,
  • FG_3,
  • FG_4.

FG_1 supports F_A.

FG_2 supports F_B.

FG_3 supports F_A and F_B.

FG_4 supports F_B and F_C.

The FunctionGroup is given as a feature, only one at a time.

In my lib.rs i would like to import the Function as modules if enabled for the function group


// here should be something like
// if group 1 enable functions A
// if group 2 enable functions B
// if group 3 enable functions A+B
// if group 4 enable functions B+C

// some conditional that enables mod ...
// #[cfg(feature = "F_A")
// #[cfg(F_A)
mod MyModF_A;

// some conditional that enables mod ...
// #[cfg(feature = "F_B")
// #[cfg(F_B)
mod MyModF_B;

// some conditional that enables mod ...
// #[cfg(feature = "F_C")
// #[cfg(F_C)
mod MyModF_C;

I have tried to use Cargo Features

[features]

F_A = []
F_B = []
F_C = []

FG_1 = ["F_A"]
FG_2 = ["F_B"]
FG_3 = ["F_A", "F_B"]
FG_4 = ["F_B", "F_C"]

But this allows users of my lib to enable function F_A, F_B, F_C directly, which is not suitable.

Is there a (simple) way to do this ?

Task: Setting up customized value to GST_PLUGIN_PATH to GStreamer in Rust

I'm creating an ABR controller for streaming user's desktop using Rust and GStreamer. The purpose of my abrcontroller.rs is to set up a GStreamer instance and handle bitrate changes. The sink I'm redirecting the stream requires fragmented mp4.

I can see the stream fine (with a constant bitrate) using gst-launch-1.0 with the parameters defined in starting_string (and piping it to the sink). However, with the current piece of code I can reach tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Launching gstreamer pipeline");, but right after that I'm getting following error:

Error: failed to initialize cmaf from input

Caused by:
    0: mp4 error: unexpected eof
    1: unexpected eof

I got the error earlier as well when launching the stream using gst-launch-1.0 in terminal, and fixed it by setting GST_PLUGIN_PATH to the folder containing my plugins. Therefore, I thought that setting it as an environmental value to the code would work, but it does not seem to do (or the issue is elsewhere).

Any ideas what might be the issue? I'm running Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS, the version of my gst-launch-1.0 is 1.24.2, and I have defined glib = "0.21.1" and gstreamer = "0.24.0" in my Cargo.toml.

The GStream initializing code is following:

...
    pub async fn run(mut self) -> anyhow::Result<()> {

        tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Setting GST_PLUGIN_PATH environment variable");
        env::set_var(
            "GST_PLUGIN_PATH",
            "/path/to/gst-plugins-rs/target/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/debug"
        );

        tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Initializing gstreamer");
        gstreamer::init()?;

        tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Setting parameters");
        let strating_string = "\
            --quiet \
            ximagesrc use-damage=0 ! video/x-raw,framerate=25/1 ! \
            videoconvert ! queue ! \
            x264enc tune=zerolatency speed-preset=veryfast key-int-max=30 bitrate=8000 vbv-buf-capacity=1000 ! \
            h264parse ! \
            isofmp4mux fragment-duration=4000000 ! \
            fdsink fd=1
        ";

        tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Launching gstreamer pipeline");
        let pipeline = gstreamer::parse::launch(strating_string)?.dynamic_cast::<gstreamer::Pipeline>().expect("(abrcontroller.rs) Pipeline expected");
        tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Setting up an encoder");
        let encoder = pipeline.by_name("encoder").expect("(abrcontroller.rs) Encoder element not found");

        tracing::info!("(abrcontroller.rs) Setting pipeline state to playing");
        pipeline.set_state(gstreamer::State::Playing);
...

Edit: I removed --quiet \ from the code, and got following error:

...
2025-09-26T12:45:09.260904Z  INFO moq_abr_controller::abrcontroller: (abrcontroller.rs) Setting up an encoder

thread 'main' panicked at moq-abr-controller/src/abrcontroller.rs:47:51:
(abrcontroller.rs) Encoder element not found
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
2025-09-26T12:45:09.261322Z DEBUG moq_lite::session::subscriber: subscribe cancelled broadcast=abr track=seconds id=0
Error: failed to initialize cmaf from input

Caused by:
    0: mp4 error: unexpected eof
    1: unexpected eof

Edit 2: I was able to solve the issue by copying debug folder from /path/to/gst-plugins-rs/target/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu to /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/gstreamer-1.0, removed --quiet (well, it shouldn't matter at this point) and added name=encoder parameter to x264enc.

I'm not sure if copying those self-compiled files was the best practice (but at least gst-inspect-1.0 isofmp4mux finds the module). Therefore, I'm leaving this question open.

The modified strating_string is now following:

"\
            ximagesrc use-damage=0 ! video/x-raw,framerate=25/1 ! \
            videoconvert ! queue ! \
            x264enc name=encoder tune=zerolatency speed-preset=veryfast key-int-max=30 bitrate=8000 vbv-buf-capacity=1000 ! \
            h264parse ! \
            isofmp4mux fragment-duration=4000000 ! \
            fdsink fd=1
        "

Task: Rust core::arch::asm throwing error where type u64 doesn't fit into class reg

I have recently been learning how to program bare metal to write a kernal for the rpi4b. The last thing I needed to add was a way to have the main script only run on one core. The method I decided to go with is to use inline assembly. This is the code that is throwing the error:

fn get_cpu_id() -> u64 {
    let x: u64;
    unsafe { core::arch::asm!("mrs {}, MPIDR_EL1", out(reg) x) }; //get the core number
    x & 0b11 //check if its the right core
}

This is the error that is being thrown:

error: type `u64` cannot be used with this register class
  --> src/main.rs:45:61
   |
45 |     unsafe { core::arch::asm!("mrs {}, MPIDR_EL1", out(reg) x) };
   |                                                             ^
   |
   = note: register class `reg` supports these types: i8, i16, i32, f16, f32

I did try using i32 instead of u64 but x really needs to be u64 because according to A64 mrs takes the <systemreg> (which in my case is MPIDR_ELI) makes a copy as a u64 and puts it into a <Xt> general register (for me, x). When I tried using i32 it also threw an error:

error: invalid operand for instruction
  --> src/main.rs:45:32
   |
45 |     unsafe { core::arch::asm!("mrs {}, MPIDR_EL1", out(reg) x) };
   |                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
note: instantiated into assembly here
  --> <inline asm>:1:10
   |
1  |     mrs r0, MPIDR_EL1
   |             ^

error: Failed to parse crate metadata

I believe this is due to the size issue but I'm not sure. I would prefer to not use a boot.S file but if I can't get this to work that would be my next step. I have also checked the Rust docs on inline assembly and noted the section on explicit register operands but have so far failed to find the correct ones for aarch64. If anyone has any ideas they would be much appreciated.

--edit--

based on Fuz's reply I thought it might be wise to add my .cargo/config.toml and cargo.toml files:

[target.'cfg(all(target_arch = "aarch64", target_os = "none"))']

[build]

[package]
name = "kerntest"

[[bin]]

path = "src/main.rs"
